metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing strategy (e.g. dual-format verification window, coordinated deploy order, or a revert recipe) to cover the gap between this story landing and e165/3183 landing.
findings.finding: The plan changes `author_sig` from raw canonical bytes (existing bde5 behavior) to a DSSE-encoded in-toto Statement, which is a breaking format change for any consumer that currently reads or verifies `author_sig`. The Scope section names era model (e165) and ledger/verdict (3183) as out-of-scope follow-on stories, but the plan provides no expand-contract sequencing, feature flag, or rollback/back-out path to keep the system functional between this story landing and those stories landing.
findings.location: Scope section; Why section; file_impact _seam.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_commands/verify_authorship.py` to `file_impact` (or explicitly sequence the 3183 update as a hard prerequisite), and describe how `_verify_signed` will resolve the Statement payload vs. raw-bytes mismatch when classifying new events.
findings.finding: The scope-exclusion claim 'OUT: ledger/verdict (3183 transition), which CONSUMES this story's content_hash + verify' is a false exclusion: verify_authorship.py already contains a hardcoded content-binding check (line 127: `envelope.payload != ev.payload`, where ev.payload is raw canonical bytes recomputed at line 203-205) that will immediately return BAD_SIGNATURE for every event signed under the new Statement payload format this story introduces. The file is not in file_impact and is explicitly excluded, yet the code change is load-bearing for it.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Out: era model (e165 transition) and ledger/verdict (3183 transition)'; verify_authorship.py:114–138 (especially lines 127, 203–205)
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add verify_authorship.py to file_impact and update _verify_signed to extract content_hash from the Statement and re-derive the expected canonical bytes for comparison, or (b) explicitly gate this story on the 3183 update landing first (reverse the sequencing), since the two changes are mutually load-bearing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_commands/verify_authorship.py` to `file_impact` and describe how its payload content-binding check (lines 200–205, 127) must be updated to match the new in-toto Statement subject (`content_hash`) rather than raw canonical bytes.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/_commands/verify_authorship.py` is an unenumerated consumer that directly implements the OLD raw-bytes payload binding and will break when `sign_authorship` switches to an in-toto Statement payload.
findings.location: Scope / file_impact (absent entry); `src/rebar/_commands/verify_authorship.py` lines 200–205, 127
findings.suggested_fix: In `verify_authorship.py`, replace the raw-bytes `payload` reconstruction with a `content_hash` computation matching what `sign_authorship` embeds in the Statement subject, then check that the decoded Statement's subject `content_hash` equals that computed value instead of comparing `envelope.payload` directly to raw bytes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_commands/verify_authorship.py` to `file_impact` and update `_verify_signed`'s content-binding check so it extracts and verifies `content_hash` from the Statement subject rather than comparing `envelope.payload` against raw event bytes.
findings.finding: verify_authorship.py's _verify_signed re-derives ev.payload as raw canonical event bytes and guards on envelope.payload == ev.payload (line 127); after the transition the envelope's payload is an in-toto Statement, so this guard will always fail for every live signed event, classifying them all as BAD_SIGNATURE. The file is absent from file_impact.
findings.location: file_impact section (absence of verify_authorship.py); cross-ref src/rebar/_commands/verify_authorship.py:127,200-206
findings.suggested_fix: In _verify_signed, after decoding the envelope, parse the Statement payload (JSON), extract subject[0].digest (or the content_hash field), recompute the expected content_hash from the event's canonical bytes minus author_sig, and compare those — instead of the current envelope.payload == ev.payload byte comparison.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact in-toto Statement JSON schema the authorship payload uses: specify `_type` URI (e.g. `https://in-toto.io/Statement/v0.1`), the `subject` array element shape (`{name: str, digest: {sha256: hex}}`), which field carries `event_uuid` (e.g. `subject[0].name`), which carries `content_hash` (e.g. `subject[0].digest.sha256`), and whether `predicateType`/`predicate` are included.
findings.finding: The in-toto Statement JSON schema is referenced throughout the plan ('Statement', 'subject', 'subject binds {event_uuid, content_hash}') but its field vocabulary is never defined — the _type/_statement_type URI, the subject array element structure ({name, digest: {algName: value}}), whether a predicateType/predicate is required, and which field carries content_hash vs. event_uuid are all unspecified.
findings.location: ## What — 'sign_authorship builds the Statement + content_hash'; AC line 1 'payload is an in-toto Statement … with a subject binding {event_uuid, content_hash}'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Statement shape' subsection to ## What, e.g.: `{"_type": "https://in-toto.io/Statement/v0.1", "subject": [{"name": "<event_uuid>", "digest": {"sha256": "<content_hash_hex>"}}], "predicateType": "application/vnd.rebar.authorship.v1+json", "predicate": {}}`; then reference this schema from both AC lines 1 and 2.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_commands/verify_authorship.py` to `file_impact` and specify how its payload reconstruction changes: instead of comparing `envelope.payload` to raw canonical event bytes, it must parse the Statement from `envelope.payload` and compare the Statement's `content_hash` subject field against a freshly-computed hash of the event-minus-author_sig.
findings.finding: `verify_authorship.py` is absent from `file_impact` but MUST change: it currently recomputes `payload` as raw canonical bytes (line 203) and compares `envelope.payload != ev.payload` (line 127). When the envelope payload becomes an in-toto Statement, the Statement JSON bytes ≠ raw canonical event bytes, so this content-binding check will always return BAD_SIGNATURE for every newly-signed event.
findings.location: ## file_impact (lists authorship.py, _seam.py, identity.py — omits verify_authorship.py); verify_authorship.py lines 127, 201–205

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a migration/compatibility section that addresses: (1) backward-compatible `verify_authorship` that detects and handles both old raw-bytes and new DSSE formats during a transition window, (2) a batched/idempotent backfill job for existing `author_sig` rows, (3) a documented post-rollback schema state asserting which rows remain verifiable, and (4) the dual-write window strategy during staged rollout.
findings.finding: The plan changes `author_sig` from a raw-bytes signature to a DSSE-encoded in-toto Statement envelope (a persisted format change on event rows), but provides no migration, backfill, or backward-compatibility strategy for existing rows written with the old format. `verify_authorship` is specified to 'parse the Statement', implying it will reject all pre-existing author_sig values, with no rollback path and no statement of the data state after a partial deployment.
findings.location: ## What — `sign_authorship`/`verify_authorship` description; `author_sig` stamp in `_seam.py`; Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that exercises the `_seam.py` write path end-to-end: create an event through the seam, retrieve the stored `author_sig`, decode it as a DSSE envelope, and assert `verify_authorship` returns True — confirming the seam cutover is complete and correct.
findings.finding: The `_seam.py` cutover — stamping `author_sig` with the new DSSE envelope — is a live code-path change, but no AC exercises a full write-then-read round-trip through the seam to confirm the new author_sig is both written correctly and verifiable end-to-end. All four ACs test `sign_authorship` and `verify_authorship` in unit isolation; none triggers `_seam.py` to write an event and then verifies the resulting author_sig via `verify_authorship`.
findings.location: What section: 'The write seam (_seam.py) stamps author_sig = DSSE-encoded Statement envelope'; Scope: 'In: … author_sig on the write seam'
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '[ ] Writing an event via the seam produces an `author_sig` that decodes as a valid DSSE envelope and passes `verify_authorship` against the signing identity (integration test covering `_seam.py`).`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the authorship Statement's subject schema: either align it with the in-toto v1 spec (`_type` field + `subject: [{name, digest: {<alg>: <hexval>}}]`) and the in-repo `verified_at_sha_subject()` precedent, or explicitly document why a custom subject structure is a conscious, justified departure.
findings.finding: The plan's authorship Statement subject uses a custom `{event_uuid, content_hash}` structure that diverges from both the in-toto v1 Statement spec (`subject` entries must be `{name: string, digest: map<alg, hexval>}` with a `_type` field) and from the in-repo precedent in `signing.verified_at_sha_subject()` which produces a conforming `{subject:[{name, digest:{sha1}}], predicateType}` shape. The plan does not acknowledge or justify this structural departure, leaving the 'in-toto Statement' claim potentially non-conforming.
findings.location: What section: 'The Statement's subject binds {event_uuid, content_hash}'
findings.suggested_fix: Map event_uuid → name and content_hash → digest: {sha256: <hex>} to produce a conforming in-toto v1 subject entry, matching the shape already established by signing.verified_at_sha_subject(). Add the required _type field ('https://in-toto.io/Statement/v1'). This makes the authorship Statement a genuine in-toto Statement and keeps the future DSSE-swap path open.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact in-toto Statement subject layout — e.g. `[{"name": "<event_uuid>", "digest": {"sha256": "<content_hash>"}}]` — so the executor does not have to interpret the in-toto spec independently.
findings.finding: The in-toto Statement subject schema is under-specified: in-toto subjects require a `name` string and a `digest` map (e.g. `{"sha256": "..."}` per the in-toto attestation spec). The plan says the subject 'binds {event_uuid, content_hash}' but does not clarify which field maps to `name` and which to `digest`, or whether both appear as digest entries.
findings.location: What section: 'The Statement's subject binds {event_uuid, content_hash}'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a JSON skeleton to the What section showing the Statement structure: `{"_type": "https://in-toto.io/Statement/v0.1", "subject": [{"name": "<event_uuid>", "digest": {"sha256": "<content_hash>"}}], "predicateType": "application/vnd.rebar.authorship.v1+json", "predicate": {...}}`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact in-toto Statement subject shape in the 'What' section: state whether `content_hash` is stored as a bare hex string or as an in-toto DigestSet (`{"sha256": "<hex>"}`) and where `event_uuid` sits (subject `name` field vs. a separate Statement predicate field), so AC1's structural assertion is unambiguous and downstream consumers can be written consistently.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the digest format for content_hash inside the in-toto Statement subject. The in-toto v1 spec requires a DigestSet ({"sha256": "<hex>"}) as the subject's digest map, but the plan just says 'content_hash' without format. If the implementer uses a bare hex string vs. a DigestSet, downstream consumers (e165, 3183) and the AC test will disagree on structure.
findings.location: What section: 'subject binding {event_uuid, content_hash}'; Acceptance Criteria AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Example clarification: 'subject = [{"name": event_uuid, "digest": {"sha256": <hex-of-canonical-event-minus-author_sig>}}]; content_hash is the sha256 hex value stored in digest.sha256.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the content_hash algorithm and encoding (e.g. 'sha256 hex digest of `canonical_bytes(event excluding author_sig)` using `rebar._store.canonical.canonical_bytes`') so both the signer (_seam.py) and verifier (verify_authorship, era model, ledger) derive byte-identical values.
findings.finding: The content_hash algorithm and encoding are never specified. The plan says 'content_hash = a digest of the event's canonical content EXCLUDING author_sig' but omits the hash algorithm (sha256? sha512?), encoding (hex? base64?), and which canonical serialization to apply (canonical_str? raw UTF-8?).
findings.location: ## What — 'content_hash = a digest of the event's canonical content EXCLUDING author_sig'; AC line 2 'FAILS on a tampered event (payload or content_hash mismatch)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ## What: 'content_hash = sha256 hex digest of `canonical_bytes({k: v for k, v in event.items() if k != "author_sig"})` (using `rebar._store.canonical.canonical_bytes`)' — this aligns with the existing _seam.py payload construction and makes the algorithm derivable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback classification for a valid DSSE envelope whose payload cannot be parsed as a well-formed in-toto Statement (e.g. classify as BAD_SIGNATURE), and state whether pre-transition raw-bytes-payload events should be accepted, rejected, or handled via a migration path.
findings.finding: No fallback is specified for the Statement-parsing sub-step inside `verify_authorship` when the envelope payload is present but is not a valid in-toto Statement (e.g. valid base64-decoded JSON that lacks a `subject` field, or has a malformed digest). The plan says 'parses the Statement, checks subject.event_uuid + content_hash' but defines no error classification for a structurally invalid Statement.
findings.location: ## What — 'verify_authorship parses the Statement, checks subject.event_uuid + content_hash match the event, THEN verifies the DSSE signature'; AC line 2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a body-section description of the Tier-0 fallback in `_seam.py`: what triggers it (key lookup failure / agent unavailable), what value `author_sig` carries in the unsigned case, and how it differs from the signed path.
findings.finding: AC3 ('An unavailable signing key yields an unsigned (Tier-0) write, never a blocked write') has no corresponding description in the plan body. The 'What' section describes the write seam stamping author_sig but never mentions the Tier-0 fallback path, its trigger condition, or how the unsigned state is represented.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC3; What section / _seam.py description
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, under the _seam.py bullet, add: 'If the signing key is unavailable (e.g. key lookup raises / agent not running), the seam writes author_sig = null (or an explicit sentinel) and records write_tier = 0; the write is never blocked.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC1/AC2) that asserts the old raw-bytes signing path is absent — e.g. 'sign_authorship output is NOT a bare byte string / does not verify as a raw SSH signature over canonical event bytes'.
findings.finding: The plan is a migration (raw-bytes → in-toto Statement) but no acceptance criterion verifies removal or non-existence of the old raw-bytes signing path. AC1 and AC2 verify the new behavior only; if the old code path survives alongside the new one, the migration is incomplete and undiscoverable by the test suite.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC1, AC2; What section authorship.py description

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the DSSE+in-toto Statement architectural decision (including the `payloadType` constant's canonical definition location and versioning policy) in an ADR or CLAUDE.md update, referenced from the plan.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a significant architectural pivot — from signing raw canonical bytes to a DSSE-wrapped in-toto Statement — that downstream stories (e165, 3183) will depend on, but no ADR or design-doc update is planned to capture this decision or the versioned payloadType string contract.
findings.location: ## What / ## Scope / Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `docs/adr/NNNN-authorship-dsse-intoto.md` (or equivalent) that records: (1) why DSSE+in-toto over raw bytes, (2) the canonical location of the `AUTHORSHIP_PAYLOAD_TYPE = 'application/vnd.rebar.authorship.v1+json'` constant (e.g., a shared constants module), and (3) the versioning policy. Reference it in the plan under a 'Documentation' section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate which existing test files / test cases from the Gerrit stack (e.g., change 561's authorship tests) exercise the old raw-bytes sign/verify contract and explicitly declare them as in-scope to rewrite under this story.
findings.finding: The plan changes sign_authorship and verify_authorship from signing raw canonical bytes (the delivered bde5/change-561 contract) to an in-toto Statement/DSSE envelope, but does not acknowledge or enumerate the callers — specifically the tests in the existing Gerrit stack (558/560/561/563/576/579/580/581) — that depend on the old format and will break or require updating.
findings.location: ## Why / ## Scope / file_impact
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Test impact' subsection to file_impact listing the affected test module(s) (e.g., `tests/attest/test_authorship.py`) and stating they must be updated to assert the new DSSE/in-toto structure, replacing assertions on raw-bytes signatures.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the write seam stamps author_sig atomically with the event write (e.g., single DB transaction or single object construction before persistence) and define the idempotency contract for re-entrant seam calls on the same event.
findings.finding: The atomicity of stamping `author_sig` onto the event in the write seam is unspecified: if the canonical event is written first and author_sig is patched in a second write, there is a TOCTOU window where an event exists without a signature, and a retry could double-sign with a different content_hash.
findings.location: ## What — 'The write seam (_seam.py) stamps author_sig = DSSE-encoded Statement envelope'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the write-seam bullet: 'The canonical event object (including author_sig) is fully constructed in memory before any persistence call, so no partial-write window exists; if the seam is called twice on the same event_uuid, the second call is a no-op (idempotent).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or explicit test requirement) that directly validates the author_sig exclusion: assert that re-stamping `author_sig` on an otherwise-identical event leaves `content_hash` unchanged, AND that mutating any other event field produces a different `content_hash`.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a critical security invariant — content_hash is computed over canonical event bytes EXCLUDING `author_sig` — but no AC tests this exclusion property directly. A test that mutates only `author_sig` and asserts content_hash is unchanged (and conversely that mutating any other field changes content_hash) is required to verify the exclusion logic is correct. Without it, a bug that accidentally includes `author_sig` in the hash would pass all stated ACs.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 / What section (content_hash definition)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: 'A test asserts that `content_hash` is invariant under changes to `author_sig` alone, and changes when any other event field is mutated, confirming the exclusion boundary.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the epic AC requires `create_placeholder` as the literal public symbol name; if it is only an internal implementation step, remove the alias and satisfy the AC directly through `ensure_identity_for` (already exported in `rebar.__all__`).
findings.finding: `create_placeholder` is a Rule-of-Three violation and surface proliferation: the plan proposes it as a pure alias over the already-public `ensure_identity_for`, which has 8+ existing call-sites and is in `rebar.__all__`, while `create_placeholder` has zero current call-sites.
findings.location: ## What — 'Add the epic-AC-named `create_placeholder(provider, external_id, display_name)` path (alias over the delivered `ensure_identity_for`) so the AC surface exists.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Check whether `signing.verified_at_sha_subject` (or a generalization of it) can serve as the shared in-toto Statement factory for authorship, or explicitly document why the authorship Statement shape requires a separate builder.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new in-toto Statement constructor in `authorship.py` without checking whether `signing.verified_at_sha_subject` (which already produces an in-toto-shaped `{subject, predicateType}` dict) can be generalized or reused, risking a second parallel Statement-builder in the same codebase.
findings.location: ## What — 'sign_authorship builds the Statement + content_hash'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `rebar/__init__.py` and `src/rebar/_lib_writes.py` to `file_impact` (or document that `create_placeholder` is internal-only), since the new alias must be exported through the same chain as `ensure_identity_for` for the AC surface to be reachable.
findings.finding: `create_placeholder` does not exist anywhere in the codebase — it is a new symbol the plan adds as an alias over `ensure_identity_for`, but the plan names it as if it is an existing surface to be wired up. This is a new export that also needs to be added to `rebar/__init__.py` and `_lib_writes.py` for the AC surface to be publicly accessible.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC4 / file_impact: `src/rebar/_commands/identity.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/__init__.py` and `src/rebar/_lib_writes.py` to the `file_impact` list with the note that `create_placeholder` needs to be re-exported alongside `ensure_identity_for`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify `create_placeholder` as a distinct entry point vs. the already-public `rebar.ensure_identity_for`, or replace AC4 with a test that verifies `ensure_identity_for` satisfies the epic AC directly (avoiding a redundant alias).
findings.finding: The plan introduces `create_placeholder(provider, external_id, display_name)` as a new public AC surface and describes it as 'an alias over the delivered `ensure_identity_for`', but `ensure_identity_for` is already fully public (`rebar.ensure_identity_for`), has 8+ call sites across tests and production code, and does exactly what `create_placeholder` would do. No prior-art search or Rule-of-Three justification is provided for why a second named entry point is needed rather than directing callers to the existing one.
findings.location: What section: 'Add the epic-AC-named create_placeholder(provider, external_id, display_name) path'; AC4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the content_hash algorithm explicitly in the What section (e.g. 'SHA-256 of the UTF-8-encoded canonical JSON bytes, hex-encoded') so the executor and downstream stories can align without a clarifying question.
findings.finding: The hash algorithm used to compute content_hash is not stated. An implementer cannot choose a hash function from 'digest of the event's canonical content EXCLUDING author_sig' alone.
findings.location: What section: 'content_hash = a digest of the event's canonical content EXCLUDING author_sig'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What bullet: 'content_hash = SHA-256 hex digest of the canonical event bytes EXCLUDING the author_sig field'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for each AC (e.g. `pytest tests/attest/test_authorship.py -k 'test_sign_dsse_structure or test_verify_wrong_identity or test_tamper or test_unsigned_tier or test_create_placeholder' -v`) so done-ness is deterministically verifiable.
findings.finding: No concrete proving commands are given for any AC — all four use 'a test asserts …' without naming a test file, test function, or a `pytest -k` pattern. This means done-ness cannot be confirmed from the plan alone.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (all four ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Append to each AC a parenthetical like `(pytest tests/attest/test_authorship.py::test_<name> -v)` naming the specific test that exercises it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add structured logging (at minimum) for each new failure point on the write and verify paths — DSSE construction failure, content_hash computation, and subject-binding mismatch — so operators can observe and debug authorship gaps.
findings.finding: The plan adds new failure points on the write path (DSSE envelope construction, content_hash computation over canonical event bytes, subject-binding verification) with no instrumentation plan — no metric, log, or trace is mentioned for these paths.
findings.location: ## What — sign_authorship / verify_authorship / _seam.py stamp
findings.suggested_fix: In `sign_authorship` and `_seam.py`, emit a structured log entry (e.g., `logger.warning('authorship.sign_failed', reason=..., event_uuid=...)`) on any exception that causes fallback to Tier-0; in `verify_authorship`, emit `logger.error('authorship.verify_failed', reason=..., event_uuid=...)` on subject-binding mismatch or signature failure rather than raising a bare exception.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sentence to the 'Why' (or 'What') section stating the positive rationale for the in-toto Statement approach: specifically, that binding `event_uuid` in the subject prevents cross-event signature replay and that the structured `content_hash` in a DigestSet makes the binding extractable by downstream consumers (e165, 3183) without re-deriving canonical bytes.
findings.finding: The plan's 'Why' section explains the divergence but does not state a positive rationale for why wrapping an in-toto Statement (subject with content_hash) is architecturally better than the delivered raw-bytes approach — i.e., what properties the Statement binding adds (event_uuid domain-separation, content-hash replay prevention, interop with in-toto tooling).
findings.location: Why section

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
